coredata.dc:title: Architecting Bi2S3/graphene quantum dots/TiO2 photoelectrodes for aqueous Cr(VI)/methyl orange removal
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Hongchao
Du, Pengwei
Zhang, Zhiwei
Yao, Lu
Cao, Kesheng
Li, Songtian
Sheng, Pengtao
coredata.dc:description: Ternary shell/core/core Bi2S3/graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanowire arrays (Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs) photoelectrodes are constructed through electrophoretic deposition of GQDs onto TNWs followed by electrochemical deposition of Bi2S3 QDs. An interesting synergistic effect was observed in configuration of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. Under irradiation, the photocurrent density of 50 μA/cm2 (an enhancement of ∼86%) and the removal efficiency of Cr (VI) is 97% (an enhancement of ∼76%) compared to that TiO2 nanowire array were obtained. Such significant enhancement is attributed to the strong photoabsorption ability of Bi2S3 QDs and excellent electron transport ability of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid photoelectrode
Photocatalytic reduction
Electrodeposition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and PEC performances evaluation of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs ternary hybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A, TEM image of GQDs/TNWs. B, HRTEM image of A. C, SEM image of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. Insets of C are the cross-section image of pristine TiO2 NWs and top-view image of GQDs/TNWs, respectively. D, Dark-field TEM image taken under STEM mode of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs and corresponding elemental mapping.
XRD patterns of pure TiO2 NWs and Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. A: anatase; T: titanium; B: Bi2S3.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry curves of different photoelectrodes under illumination. (b) Light on/off photocurrent densities response spectra of corresponding photoelectrodes: a, TNWs; b, GQDs/TNWs; c, Bi2S3/TNWs; d, Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. (c) Photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of methyl orange in absence (curve B)/presence (curve D) of Cr(VI), Cr(VI) in absence (curve C)/presence (curve E) of methyl orange over Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs, and Cr(VI) (curve A) over TNWs, methyl orange in presence (curve G) of Cr(VI), Cr(VI) in presence (curve F) of methyl orange over Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs after three months.

head.ce:title: Architecting Bi2S3/graphene quantum dots/TiO2 photoelectrodes for aqueous Cr(VI)/methyl orange removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Pingdingshan University, Pingdingshan 467000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Instrumentation Center, Pingdingshan University, Pingdingshan 467000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Pingdingshan University, Pingdingshan 467000, China (S. Li, P. Sheng).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Pengwei Du and Hongchao Geng contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ternary shell/core/core Bi2S3/graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanowire arrays (Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs) photoelectrodes are constructed through electrophoretic deposition of GQDs onto TNWs followed by electrochemical deposition of Bi2S3 QDs. An interesting synergistic effect was observed in configuration of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. Under irradiation, the photocurrent density of 50 μA/cm2 (an enhancement of ∼86%) and the removal efficiency of Cr (VI) is 97% (an enhancement of ∼76%) compared to that TiO2 nanowire array were obtained. Such significant enhancement is attributed to the strong photoabsorption ability of Bi2S3 QDs and excellent electron transport ability of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: There are currently increasing concerns about methyl orange/Cr(VI) as a result of their widespread distribution in various environmental media and potential adverse effects to biota and human [1]. Many methyl orange/Cr(VI) have already been proven to have endocrine disrupting effects [2]. Cr(VI) is the typical heavy metal pollutant with toxicity 300 times higher than that of Cr(III), primarily from leather tanning, textile manufacturing, electroplating [3]. Cr(VI) can cause serious health problems such as cancer, pulmonary congestion and liver damage [4,5]. The United States has set a new standard of Cr(VI) for 10 µg/L in 2014.

The usual method for removing Cr(VI) is physical adsorption, ion exchange, chemical precipitation and photocatalytic reduction [6]. Photocatalysis is an efficient, active and clean technology for many organic pollutants and heavy metal ions in aqueous media in view of solar energy conversion [7]. Wang et al. [8–9] fabricated a core-shell structure of polyaniline coated protonic titanate nanobelt composites for both Cr(VI) and humic acid removal, also, they successfully established the relationship between the graphene oxide colloidal behavior and the co-adsorption of Cd(II) and phosphate. Here, ternary Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs nanocomposite was designed and fabricated via successively electrophoretic and pulse electrodeposition deposition of GQDs and Bi2S3 QDs onto the TNWs, respectively. For evaluating the activity of the as-prepared ternary composite, the photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity was examined by reducing Cr(VI)/Methyl Orang contaminated waste water.

Electrophoretic deposition was used to prepare the GQDs/TNWs nanocomposite in a three-electrode cell using the GQD aqueous solution (pH = 8) as an electrolyte, TNWs as working electrode, platinum sheet as counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode as reference [10]. +6 V bias potential was applied to the TNWs for 2 h. Upon depositing, GQDs/TNWs were rinsed with significant amounts of deionized water.

The GQDs/TNWs were modified with Bi2S3 nanoparticles in a solution containing 10 mM bismuth nitrate, 50 mM sodium thiosulfate and 10 mM sodium citrate. A rectangular potential pulsed mode in a three-electrode system was applied with the GQDs/TNWs as working electrode, a platinum sheet as counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode as reference [11]. For pulse electrodeposition, a potential of −1.0 V was applied for 0.1 s, following by a potential of 0 V for 1 s. The number of consecutive pulse sequence was 500 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and PEC performances evaluation of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs ternary hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image of GQDs/TNWs are shown in Fig. 1 A, one can see that TNWs were well covered by GQDs. However, the corresponding lattice structure of GQDs can not be well distinguished Fig. 1B. The shapes of the pure TNWs and the hybrid Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs were characterized by FE-SEM as shown in Fig. 1C. Obviously, the TNWs film is highly smooth porous network and the top is loosely arranged with an average diameter and length of 40 nm and 4 μm, respectively. The diameters of the TiO2 nanowires are so small that they tend to bend with other nanowires as their length increases. After the electrophoretic deposition, the surface of the TiO2 nanowires turns uneven, Fig. 1C. Dark-field TEM image taken under STEM mode of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs and corresponding elemental mapping were also displayed in Fig. 1D. As we expected, Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs consist of the elements O, Ti, Bi, S and C, Fig. 1D. In addition, Bi and S are the major elements and small amounts of element C that is identified at 0.277 keV. TEM image and spectral data of GQDs are shown in Fig. S1

The XRD patterns of pristine TNWs and Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs are shown in Fig. 2 . The typical peaks at 25.28°, 48.3°, 62.8° and 76.27° can be ascribed to the (1 0 1), (2 0 0), (2 0 4) and (3 0 1) planes of anatase [12]. No characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs was detected for Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs, which can be explained by the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of GQDs in Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. Apart from the peaks of TiO2 and Ti substrate, the additional diffraction peaks can be attributed to the orthorhombic phase of Bi2S3 (JCPDS NO. 17-0320) from Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs.

The light absorption ability and charges transfer/recombination mechanism of hybrid photoelectrode are displayed in Fig. S2. The linear sweep voltammetry measurements of different photoelectrodes were shown in Fig. 3 a. With the increase of bias potential, the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes is hindered, and the transfer of electrons to the external circuit can be also accelerated. Compared with pure TNWs, the onset potential of GQDs/TNWs, Bi2S3/TNWs, Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs reveals a gradually slight shift from −0.72 V (vs. SCE) for pristine TNWs to −0.75 V (vs. SCE) for Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs. The higher photocurrent density and lower onset potential demonstrate more efficient charge separation and transport in the Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs.

The transient photocurrent densities of the as-prepared electrodes were also assessed and shown in Fig. 3b. Obviously, the photoelectrodes show relatively good reproducibility and stability as the illumination was turned on and off. Compared with pure TNWs, the photocurrent densities of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs, Bi2S3/TNWs, and GQDs/TNWs increase by 186%, 115%, and 38%, respectively.

Notably, the increased photocurrent density of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs is higher than the sum of Bi2S3/TNWs and GQDs/TNWs, which sufficiently indicates a synergetic effect in the ternary Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs, and the outstanding electron transporter of GQDs middle layers.

It is known that the key point in photocatalytic applications is to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the utilization efficiency of light quantum. As we know, photocatalytic oxidation of methyl orange needs to consume photogenerated holes while photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) needs to accept the photogenerated electrons. In addition, Cr(VI) is an oxidant under acidic conditions which can assist the degradation of methyl orange. Therefore, it is expected to achieve an ideally simultaneous detoxification of methyl orange and Cr(VI). In this study, a 20 mL simulated wastewater, containing 20 mg L−1 methyl orange and 20 mg L−1 Cr(VI) was purified within 80 min.

Detoxification efficiencies of Cr(VI) in control experiments are shown in Fig. 3c. The removal efficiency in coexisting system is 97% over Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs and 92% without methyl orange in solution. The removal efficiency on TNWs is 50%. Meanwhile, methyl orange removal efficiency in coexisting system is about 52% over Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs and 43% without Cr(VI) in solution. As we excepted, the existence of Cr(VI) indeed accelerate the degradation of methyl orange, and the photoelectrodes display obviously detoxification efficiencies of both Cr(VI) and methyl orange. The removal efficiency of Cr(VI) is higher than that of methyl orange under same conditions. That is because it is simple for Cr(VI) to accept the photogenerated electrons to be reduced. However, photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange suffers a complex procedure to remove the substituents and destroy the benzene ring. Much more radicals are needed to deplete the intermediates to species with small molecular weights. Though, methyl orange can not be direct oxidized by Cr(VI), Cr(VI) plays an important role in methyl orange removal. First, Photogenerated electrons of photoelectrode were consumed by Cr(VI) ions reduction, which further contributes to the generation of more holes for the oxidation of target molecular. In addition, a faster oxidizing speed can be devoted by Cr(VI) by reacting with the organic acid with low molecular weight generated from methyl orange degradation.

The transfer behaviors of the photogenerated holes and electrons in Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs are proposed as follows. Since the CB and VB of Bi2S3 lie above those of GQDs and TiO2. under illumination, the electrons excited on CB of Bi2S3 would transfer to TiO2 through GQDs, the electrons in TiO2 are rapidly delivered to adsorbed oxygen to produces O2 −, which can generate hydroxyl radicals by reacting with H2O. The produced hydroxyl radicals are determinable to oxidize methyl orange. Simultaneously, Cr(VI) converts to Cr(III) by accepting the electrons. At the same time, the holes generated on TiO2 VB would transfer to Bi2S3 through GQDs, eventually induce oxidation with surface absorbed species to favoring the degradation of methyl orange. The transfer behaviors of the electrons and holes can reduce the recombination probability between them by carrying out reduction and oxidation processes in different regions, which results in the highest catalytic activity of Bi2S3/GQDs/TiO2.

Furthermore, the highly orientated TiO2 nanowire arrays are good pathway for the electron transfer. Consequently, the effective separation of the h-e pairs at Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs heterojunctions can be realized, which reduces the recombination chance of h-e pair and increases the quantum efficiencies. Methyl orange can be degraded by the hydroxyl radicals initiated by the holes and Cr(VI) can be reduced by the electrons.

The long-term storage stability and the reproducibility of composite electrode were also tested, in Fig. 3c (curve G and F). After three months' preservation, the composite electrode can still get good degradation efficiency.

In summary, the ternary composite photoelectrode of Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs was fabricated using a combined technique of controllable pulse electrodeposition and electrophoretic deposition. Bi2S3 nanoparticles effectively extend the TNWs absorption from UV to visible region. GQDs not only act as excellent electron transporter for facilitating efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, but also work as benign sensitizers for enhancing spectral absorption. In the simultaneous detoxification of Cr(VI) and methyl orange employing Bi2S3/GQDs/TNWs catalyst, the removal efficiency of 92%, 52% can be obtained.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Scientific Research Foundation for High-level Talents of Pingdingshan University (PXY-BSQD2016008, PXY-BSQD2016007), Training Programme Foundation for the National Project of Pingdingshan University (PXY-PYJJ2016003), the Key Scientific Research Program of the Higher Education Institutions of Henan Province (17A150044, 18A150043), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.11.126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Qun-yan Wu, Xiangke Wang et al.
